input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A teleoperated, 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. The physical twin comprises a lower‑level and upper‑level chassis, housing both propulsion and suspension systems, and autonomy elements that implement Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The vehicle operates in a road‑based autonomous domain, providing live vehicle‑state data such as speed, position, and object tracking, making it a suitable candidate for digital twinning due to its compact size, real‑time data acquisition, and safety‑critical ACC functionalities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin System (DTS) delivers three primary services: 1) *Data Monitoring* – a web‑based UI that displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows operators to view, save, and generate detailed reports; 2) *Computation Offloading* – offloads the ACC computation (lead‑vehicle detection, desired speed calculation) to cloud or edge servers, returning the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation; 3) *Remote Management* – enables operators to remotely activate/deactivate ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands. These services require low end‑to‑end latency communication and robust data collection/reporting to uphold safety‑critical constraints.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin synchronizes the virtual and physical subsystems to a shared notion of time with a maximum relative deviation of 100 ms. This 100 ms accuracy is mandated by hardware constraints and meets the stricter synchronization thresholds of safety‑critical vehicular standards. All ROS2 messages and sensor data timestamps are aligned to this accuracy, ensuring that time granularity for critical safety services is 100 ms and that end‑to‑end latency from sensor acquisition to actuation remains below this bound.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three functional entity instances compose the system: the Physical Twin Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity. Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware node (e.g., CAN‑bridge, edge compute node, and operator workstation), yielding a highly decentralized architecture. The distinct FEs within each entity (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking in the Physical Twin, Data Storage in the Digital Twin, 3D Visualization & Control Panel in the User Entity) operate as separate ROS2 packages but are coordinated through distributed topic exchanges, effectively forming a multi‑instance twin with clear responsibility separation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is orchestrated following ISO 23247 RA, mapping each software component to a Functional Entity (FE). Each FE is implemented as a dedicated ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes. The User Interface FE is split into two sub‑components—3D Visualization and Control Panel—both instantiated within the User Entity. Data management, control logic and pre‑processing reside in the Digital Twin Entity, while actuation and sensor interfacing are handled by the Physical Twin Entity. Inter‑entity communication is performed over ROS2 publish‑subscribe and client‑server topics, ensuring resource allocation, component integration, and system‑wide coordination across the distributed hardware deployment.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data originates from the vehicle’s LiDAR, motor, steering, servo and vehicle status modules, producing raw point‑clouds, RPM, steering angle, and telemetry samples. The raw data are first ingested by the Physical‑Twin Data‑Collection FE and immediately forwarded to the Data Translation FE, which serialises each stream into a ROS2 DDS message, aligns the message types with the ISO 23247 semantic schema, and performs real‑time unit conversion (e.g., from degrees to radians). Pre‑processing is then carried out by the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component, which applies voxel‑grid filtering, Euclidean clustering, and Kalman‑based tracking to output validated object tracks and projected trajectories. The resulting virtual state objects are published over the local ROS2 overlay to the 3D Visualization FE, where the virtual twin renders live updates at 10 Hz, guaranteeing minimal latency and data integrity through sequence‑number correlation and checksum verification.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals such as steering angle, throttle level, and servo positions are produced by the Virtual Cockpit UI and sent as ROS2 action messages over the local middleware to the Actuation FE. The Actuation FE validates the command payload (ensuring correct format and acceptable ranges) before transmitting it to the vehicle’s on‑board control system via a UDP socket over the same Wi‑Fi link used for sensor data. The on‑board system replies with a simple ACK containing the sequence identifier, which the Actuation FE forwards to the virtual twin to close the feedback loop.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All intra‑software component communication is implemented atop ROS2 using the Data‑Distribution Service (DDS) over UDP. Inter‑entity connections between the vehicle and the Digital Twin server are established over a Wi‑Fi 802.11n network using UDP sockets. Each message includes a sequence number and checksum to guarantee ordering and data integrity. The network topology is a flat, distributed structure with no dedicated encryption or VPN layers specified in the source document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture specifies an entity‑based structure comprising a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities responsible for specific tasks (e.g., data acquisition, transformation, user interface). The standard, however, does not prescribe detailed engineering methodologies, quality assurance practices, testing strategies, or version‑control mechanisms for developing or evolving the DT.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adheres to the ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing (part 2: reference architecture). The standard defines the entity hierarchy and functional responsibilities, but does not stipulate additional certifications or interoperability specifications beyond providing this reference framework. No mention of compliance with other industry standards or certification requirements is present.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The 3GPP TS 22.185 service‑requirements specification for vehicle‑to‑everything (V2X) communications mandates compliance with the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). It requires explicit user consent before any personal data is shared, mandates pseudonymisation or anonymisation of identifiers in vehicular messages, enforces data‑minimisation limits, and defines data‑sharing agreements between mobile and roadside infrastructures. Encryption of V2X traffic must use TLS 1.3 or an equivalent PKI‑based scheme, and TLS certificates must be signed by a trusted certificate authority as part of the authentication and access‑control mechanisms. The spec also recommends periodic data‑retention reviews and deletion of data after the necessary lifecycle for safety analytics, ensuring that any aggregated data used for digital‑twin model training is anonymised and stored in a secure, role‑based access environment.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: ISO 26262‑1:2018 specifies the functional‑safety framework for road vehicles, requiring hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA), classification of safety‑integrity levels (ASIL), safety concept and functional‑hazard design, and verification & validation of safety‑critical functions. The guideline stresses the implementation of a fail‑safe approach where ACC systems must detect abnormal operation, downgrade the system behaviour to a safe state, and trigger a driver alert. ISO 15622 further requires performance‑test procedures for adaptive cruise‑control systems, including validation of maintaining a minimum safe following distance under diverse traffic densities and emergency braking scenarios, with a statistical confidence level of 99.9%. These safety standards lay the foundation for secure digital‑twin architectures, while 3GPP TS 22.185 mandates cybersecurity protocols such as authentication, authorization, and encryption to protect V2X data exchanges.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
